courses.prerequisites: Admission to degree. NSC level 5 in Mathematics and level 4 in English HL (or level 5 in English FAL).
courses.course_outline: An introductory course in accounting aimed at all Commerce students who are not required to complete specialised accounting courses, and also open to students in other faculties seeking an introduction to the accounting discipline. The course provides Commerce graduates with the necessary acumen to engage in a managerial context with the accounting function within their organisations. Topics include a basic introduction to financial accounting and published financial statements, and management accounting. Commerce students are also required to complete ACC1022Z.
courses.lecture_times: 4 lectures a week, Mondays to Thursdays. Tutorials: a double-period tutorial per week.
courses.dp_requirements: 75% course participation and a minimum of 38% for the April test.
courses.assessment: One class test in April. One final exam as part of the June exam block.

courses.prerequisites: Matriculation mathematics, Mathematics Literacy, or registration for the Postgraduate Diploma in Management in Entrepreneurship, Marketing, Sport and Business Communication.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students with little or no commercial or financial background to the fundamentals of managerial finance. Students will develop a basic understanding of accounting concepts, the ability to read financial statements and perform basic ratio analysis, an understanding of time value of money, basic steps toward efficient working capital management, and a basic understanding of capital budgeting and valuations. Key topics include: introduction to basic accounting concepts; understanding annual financial statements; sources of finance; basic financial ratio analysis; investments, risk and return; working capital management; cost volume profit analysis; budgeting; time value of money; basic valuations; cost of capital; discounted cash flow; and capital budgeting.
courses.notes: Disclaimer: This course will be run in 2026 unless fewer than 20 students sign up for it.
courses.dp_requirements: Writing all class tests. Attendance and submission of 80% of tutorials. Satisfactory completion of the project and assignments. 40% average year mark.
courses.assessment: Class test 1 – 15%; class test 2 – 15%; objective tests (10% – best 5 out of 10); 1 group project (10%); final examination (50%).

courses.course_outline: The course introduces students to the main features of business intelligence and business analytics, including data warehousing and data marts, decision support systems, OLAP, data mining and analytics, corporate performance management, data visualisation, real-time BI, pervasive BI, mobile BI and big data analytics. Case studies and management approaches for implementation are covered, and a hands-on project requires students to produce a management report after analysing data using commercial BI software.
courses.lecture_times: Course runs only for 3 weeks: Monday to Wednesday 5th period, Friday 4th and 5th period.
courses.assessment: Assessment requirements for both INF2006F and INF2007F need to be met in order to pass INF2008F.

courses.prerequisites: INF1003F or equivalent, or INF1003F as co-requisite.
courses.course_outline: A half course designed for students intending to major in Information Systems or Computer Science for the BCom, BBusSci or BSc degrees (students pursuing other computing degrees may be accepted, space permitting). The course explores the role of the Systems Analyst in business, different approaches used in the development of information systems, and the various tools and techniques used in the specification of system requirements. It provides in-depth knowledge of the systems development process with particular emphasis on the analysis stage of the life cycle, with a strong practical component teaching students to understand and use common tools of object-oriented systems analysis, particularly UML models.
courses.lecture_times: Monday to Wednesday, 4th period. Practical workshops: Thursday 3rd & 4th periods OR 4th & 5th periods OR 8th & 9th periods.
courses.dp_requirements: Submitted at least 80% of the coursework (80% of individual deliverables and 80% of group work). Sub-minimum of 45% course year-mark.
courses.assessment: Coursework (Formative Assessment 40% + Summative Assessment 20%) and Final Examination (40%). Sub-minimum of 40% for the final examination.

courses.prerequisites: Minimum 45% final mark for [INF2007 or INF2008 or CSC2001 or equivalent] and INF2009 and [INF1003 or CSC1016 or equivalent].
courses.course_outline: This course provides in-depth knowledge of the systems development process with particular emphasis on the design and implementation stages of the life cycle. There is a strong practical component where students use object-oriented tools to design and construct a working system, building on the skills acquired in INF2009F Systems Analysis.
courses.lecture_times: Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday, 4th period. Thursday: Weekly workshop sessions 3rd–4th periods OR 4th–5th periods. Friday: Practical workshops 5th–7th periods.
courses.dp_requirements: Submit 80% of workshops and quizzes. Year-mark of 45%. Submitted all project work.
courses.assessment: Coursework 60%; exam 40%. Sub-minimum of 45% for the final exam.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in INF2003F, INF2009F and at least 45% for INF2011S.
courses.course_outline: A first-semester capstone course for students majoring in Information Systems (IS) and either Computer Science, Finance or Informatics, who wish to gain both an understanding of project management issues and experience in the execution of such projects. The course combines the theoretical elements of project management (and people management) with the practical implementation of these concepts through the completion of a team project. It integrates practical and theoretical elements from other undergraduate IS courses.
courses.notes: Students cannot be credited for this course and for INF3003W.
courses.dp_requirements: Submission of required project work and a sub-minimum of 45% for the year mark prior to writing the final examination. Satisfactory attendance at tutorials and lectures.
courses.assessment: Coursework 70%; final examination 30%. Sub-minimum of 40% for the final examination.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in INF2003F, INF2009F and INF2011S.
courses.course_outline: This course examines the role, relationship and effect IT Applications have on businesses and vice versa, with a heavy emphasis on ERP systems, business processes and Business Process Management (BPM). Students are exposed to methodologies and techniques to identify, model, measure and improve processes, and introduced to technologies that can be used as part of process improvement initiatives, including ERP systems that impact business processes. A group project allows students to apply their analytical skills to improving an existing process. Students are introduced to S/4 HANA and acquire a basic working knowledge of the application.
courses.dp_requirements: Acceptable participation in group project; 45% for the year mark; completion and submission of 80% of workshops.
courses.assessment: Classwork 65% (workshops, class exercises, test and a group project); final examination 35%. Sub-minimum of 40% for the final examination.

courses.prerequisites: INF2003F, INF2009F and at least 45% for INF2011S.
courses.course_outline: A course for students majoring in Information Systems as well as any other student wishing to gain an understanding of electronic commerce (e-Commerce) technologies and their usage in society. The course covers both theoretical e-Commerce issues and the practical skills required to develop a basic e-Commerce system, including understanding and applying key concepts, techniques and practices that form part of e-Commerce systems design, development, implementation and usage. The practical component includes planning, structuring and developing e-Commerce related web applications as well as designing the user experience (UX), culminating in a project involving developing an e-Commerce application that addresses a real business or social need.
courses.lecture_times: 12h00–13h45 Tuesday and Wednesday; and either 13h00–14h45 or 14h00–15h45 Friday.
courses.dp_requirements: Submission of tutorials, seminar and project work, and a sub-minimum of 45% for the year mark prior to writing the final examination.
courses.assessment: Coursework 70%; exam 30%. Sub-minimum of 40% for the final examination.

courses.course_title: Introduction to Electrical Engineering & Power Utilisation
courses.source_faculty: Faculty of Engineering and the Built Environment
courses.prerequisites: PHY1013F/S (MEC), MAM1021S (MEC), PHY1004W or PHY1032S (CSC), MAM1004F (>60%) and MAM1008S (>60%) or MAM1000W (CSC).
courses.course_outline: This course aims to develop an understanding of: (a) DC circuits and networks including series and parallel circuits, Kirchhoff's laws, Mesh Analysis, Superposition Theorem, Star-to-delta and Delta-to-star Transformation, Thevenin's theorem, Maximum Power Transfer theorem, DC transients in R-L and R-C circuits; (b) Fundamentals of AC including generation, waveform concepts, period, frequency, angular velocity, phase, average, peak and RMS values; (c) Single-phase AC circuits including AC through resistance, inductance and capacitance, reactance and impedance, phasors, active, reactive and apparent power, and power factor; (d) Three-phase AC systems; (e) Magnetic circuits; (f) Single-phase transformers including construction, principle of operation, EMF equation, transformation ratio, equivalent circuits, voltage regulation, losses and efficiency; (g) DC motors including principle of operation, types, speed and torque characteristics and speed control.
courses.lecture_times: Refer to official course handout document.
courses.notes: For students in the Mechanical Engineering and Mechanical & Mechatronic Engineering programmes and Computer Science.
courses.dp_requirements: Refer to the official course handout document for detailed DP requirements.
courses.assessment: Refer to the official course handout document for assessment criteria.

courses.course_title: Introduction to Analogue and Digital Electronics
courses.source_faculty: Faculty of Engineering and the Built Environment
courses.prerequisites: MAM1021F/S (MEC), PHY1013F/S (MEC), PHY1004W or PHY1032S (CSC), MAM1004F (>60%) and MAM1008S (>60%) or MAM1000W (CSC); DP for EEE2041F.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: (a) Introduction to semiconductor physics and diode basics; (b) Diode circuit model, applications and LEDs; (c) Introduction to BJTs and basic models; (d) BJT amplifier circuit (common-emitter focus); (e) Introduction to Op Amps, ideal and practical models; (f) Op-amp inverting and non-inverting applications; (g) Introduction to FETs and FET analogue applications; (h) Simple H-bridge circuits; (i) Difference between analogue and digital applications, introduction to digital electronics; (j) Digital electronics continued — Boolean algebra and logic gates; (k) FET digital applications and introduction to Flip Flops; (l) Basics of state machines and electronic instruments.
courses.lecture_times: Refer to official course handout document.
courses.notes: For students in the Mechanical Engineering and Mechanical & Mechatronic Engineering programmes and Computer Science.
courses.dp_requirements: Refer to the official course handout document for detailed DP requirements.
courses.assessment: Refer to the official course handout document for assessment criteria.

courses.course_title: Embedded Systems I for Science Students
courses.source_faculty: Faculty of Engineering and the Built Environment
courses.course_outline: This course gives Science students majoring in Computer Engineering a strong foundation in embedded systems, introducing digital system fundamentals including: information representation, Boolean algebra, logic gate behaviour, combinational and sequential digital circuits, digital building blocks and algorithmic state machines; C programming with a focus on microcontroller applications; and basic microcontroller usage including an introduction to computer architecture, general purpose input/outputs, analogue to digital convertors and basic timers.
courses.lecture_times: Refer to official course handout document.
courses.dp_requirements: Refer to the official course handout document for detailed DP requirements.
courses.assessment: Refer to the official course handout document for assessment criteria.

courses.course_title: Embedded Systems II for Science Students
courses.source_faculty: Faculty of Engineering and the Built Environment
courses.course_outline: This course focuses on embedded systems architectures, firmware and software tool stacks, building on Embedded Systems I. Part 1 (10 credits) covers: theory and practices of design and analysis through modelling and simulation of embedded systems; embedded operating systems; and methods for modelling and simulation of computer systems, including an introduction to Linux command line and source code version control. Part 2 (6 credits) introduces Hardware Description Language (HDL) programming and computer architecture fundamentals, and tools for developing gateware and simulating HDL designs. Part 1 practicals involve using a single board computer, deploying an embedded operating system, building applications using a cross-compiler tool stack, and hardware-software interfaces, culminating in Miniproject A (IoT application). Part 2 practicals involve implementing a combination logic design and developing an HDL testbench. Computer Science students complete a more demanding Miniproject B with additional software features and performance testing.
courses.lecture_times: Refer to official course handout document.
courses.dp_requirements: Refer to the official course handout document for detailed DP requirements.
courses.assessment: Refer to the official course handout document for assessment criteria.

courses.course_title: Integrated Anatomical and Physiological Sciences Part A
courses.convener: Dr J Harbron and Associate Professor D Lang
courses.prerequisites: BIO1000F, BIO1004S and CEM1000W (or equivalent courses). An average grade of 60% or more for these courses is recommended.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces the concept of integrating human physiology, anatomy, cell biology and histology. It includes the study of cells and tissues, the basic anatomy and histology of the musculoskeletal, endocrine and digestive systems, and an introduction to embryology and osteology. Physiological concepts include fluid balance, cell signalling, hormone regulation, digestion, absorption and metabolism. Practicals involve students working in small groups using computers and specialised equipment to study the physiology and histology of the abovementioned organ systems.
courses.lecture_times: Lectures: Monday to Friday, 08h00–08h45. Practicals: Mondays or Tuesdays, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: Entrance limited to 80 students. Course is under review and content may change.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at all practical sessions; 40% average in class tests; and an average of 50% for all assignments.
courses.assessment: Class tests 30%; practical write-up 15%; assignments or tutorials 5%; final examinations 50% (theory 30% + practical 20%). Sub-minimum of 40% required for both theory and practical examinations. Supplementary examinations may be offered to students with an overall score of 45–49%. An oral examination may be required for selected students.

courses.course_title: Integrated Anatomical and Physiological Sciences Part B
courses.prerequisites: HUB2019F (or approved equivalent) and CEM1000W (or approved equivalent).
courses.course_outline: The course integrates aspects of human physiology, anatomy and histology of organ systems including cardiovascular, respiratory, nervous, reproductive, urinary and immune systems. Homeostasis and regulation form the golden thread throughout the course. Homeostatic concepts covered include thermoregulation, acid-base balance, neural transduction, cardiac output and regulation, and respiration. Students are also introduced to anthropology and concepts of ageing and disease. Practicals involve studying the physiology of the nervous system, the electrical events in the contraction of cardiac muscle and the mechanics of the respiratory system, as well as examining human anatomical specimens and histology of the organ systems.
courses.lecture_times: Lectures: Monday to Friday, 08h00–08h45. Practicals: Mondays or Tuesdays, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: Entrance limited to 80 students. Course is under review and content may change.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at all practicals; 40% average in class tests; and an average of 50% for all assignments.
courses.assessment: Class tests 30%; practicals, assignments and tutorials 20%; final examinations 50% (written theory 30% + practical 20%). Sub-minimum of 40% required for both theory and practical examinations. Supplementary examinations may be offered to students with an overall score of 45–49%. An oral examination may be required for selected students.

courses.prerequisites: HUB2019F and HUB2021S or equivalent. Alternatively, a sub-minimum of 40% average for the Physiology components of HUB2017H and PTY2000S respectively (co-requisite).
courses.course_outline: Building on second-year content, this course provides insights into the integrated physiology of humans eating, exercising and sleeping to reduce risk for diseases of lifestyle and optimise physical performance. The four modules are: Energy Metabolism, Disease of Lifestyle, Sport and Exercise, and Sleep. Topics include: advanced mitochondrial physiology; energy metabolism; energy balance and obesity; heart disease and diabetes; nutrition and health; metabolism and physiology of sport and exercise; adaptation to exercise training; principles of exercise training; nutrition for sport; and physiology of sleep and circadian rhythms in health, disease and sport.
courses.lecture_times: 1st period, Monday–Friday. Practicals/Tutorials: Wednesday or Thursday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: Course is under review and content may change.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at all practicals (including tutorials and seminar presentations held during the practical time slot); 40% average in class tests; and an average of 50% for all assignments.
courses.assessment: Class tests 30%; practical and tutorial assignments 20%; final examinations 50% (written theory 30% + practical 20%). Sub-minimum of 40% required for both theory and practical examinations. Supplementary examinations may be offered to students with an overall course mark of 45–49%. An oral examination may be required for selected students.

courses.prerequisites: HUB3006F (or approved equivalent). Exceptions at the discretion of the convener.
courses.course_outline: This course offers theoretical and practical instruction on advanced concepts in neuroscience, including: embryological development and repair of the nervous system; histological and gross anatomical appearances of the brain; electrophysiology; principles of electrical and morphological brain imaging; neuronal signalling; signal transduction in sensory, motor and autonomic nervous systems; vision and pain perception; eating disorders; mechanisms of learning; and the development of memory.
courses.lecture_times: Five 45-minute lectures per week, 1st period, Monday to Friday.
courses.notes: Course is under review and content may change.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at all practicals; 40% average mark for class tests; and an average of 50% for all assignments.
courses.assessment: Class tests 30%; practicals including assignments/seminar presentation 20%; examinations (written theory and practical theory) 50%. Sub-minimum of 40% required for both theory and practical examinations. Supplementary examinations may be offered to students with an overall score of 45–49%. An oral examination may be required for selected students.

courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to bioinformatics techniques related to the processing, analysis and interpretation of high-throughput biological data. It covers the analysis of next-generation sequence data of different types (metagenomic, RNA-Seq and full genome); statistical analysis of NGS in relation to associated metadata; phylogenetic analysis of sequence data; and medical population genetics from NGS or array data. Students who complete the course will be skilled both in handling big biological datasets and in their downstream interpretation.
The research component of the degree is based on a 90-credit dissertation. The topic of the research will be based on an analysis of large data sets from Computational Biology.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination.

courses.course_title: Infectious Disease and Vaccines (Faculty of Science)
courses.convener: Professor W Burgers and Associate Professor S Hadebe
courses.prerequisites: BIO1000F, CEM1000W, MAM1004F and STA1007S or MAM1000W (or equivalent).
courses.course_outline: The course introduces students to: the burden of infectious disease in South Africa and Africa; foundational epidemiological concepts including epidemics, outbreaks and public health; the micro-organisms of importance to human health and disease (bacteria, viruses, fungi and parasites) and their classification; and the prevention, control and treatment of infectious disease, with a focus on vaccines, integrated with an introduction to the human immune system. By the end of the course, students will be able to describe biological and social determinants of health across diverse epidemiological contexts; interpret epidemiological data; classify major human pathogens and compare their transmission, pathogenesis and control strategies; explain the structure and function of the immune system; describe how vaccines work; apply knowledge from one infectious disease to analyse another; integrate concepts from virology, bacteriology and immunology; and communicate infectious disease concepts clearly in oral, written and graphic formats.
courses.lecture_times: Lectures: Monday to Friday, 2nd period. Practicals: Fridays, 14h00–17h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance of 75% practical and tutorial sessions; 40% average in weekly quizzes; and an average of 50% for all assignments.
courses.assessment: Class record 60% (weekly quizzes 20%, practical write-ups 20%, assignments 20%); one 3-hour final examination written in November 40%. Supplementary examinations may be offered to students with an overall score of 45–49%.

courses.course_title: Pathogenesis and Treatment of Infectious Diseases (Faculty of Science)
courses.convener: Doctor MR Abrahams (Division of Medical Virology) and Doctor C Moodley (Division of Medical Microbiology)
courses.course_outline: This course provides the conceptual basis for understanding pathogenic bacteria, fungi, viruses and parasites, and particularly addresses the fundamental mechanisms of how they cause disease in humans. Topics include: how successful pathogens invade the host and escape immune defences; parts of the immune system important in resisting infection; mechanisms by which pathogens evade immune responses; antimicrobial therapy for bacterial, viral, fungal and parasitic infections; knowledge of pathogen life cycles to identify treatment targets; antimicrobial resistance mechanisms and novel approaches to combat them; and the role of antimicrobial stewardship. Through blended learning, practical sessions, field visits and interaction with experts in public health and infectious diseases, students are introduced to a variety of different career avenues.
courses.lecture_times: Refer to departmental notices. Includes practical sessions, field trips and tutorials.
courses.notes: This course is offered only in the 1st semester; students who fail will need to repeat the following year.
courses.dp_requirements: 40% average in class tests or quizzes; and an average of 50% for assignments. Attendance at all practicals, field trips and tutorials. Submission of at least 80% of assignments.
courses.assessment: Class record 60% (class tests and/or quizzes 20%, practical reports and tutorials 20%, assignments 20%); one 3-hour final examination written in June 40%. Supplementary examinations may be offered to students with an overall score of 45–49%.
